data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Elite ideological delegitimation following solar eclipse and volcanic winter disrupted ritual foundations of kingship", "mechanism": "The Thera eruption (c.1600 BC, cultural memory) or a later unrecorded volcanic event, combined with the solar eclipse of 1223 BC, shattered the cosmic-ritual legitimation upon which Mycenaean wanax and Hittite Labarna kingship depended; palace economies required performative redistribution tied to divine favor, and when ritual failure became interpretable, competing elites abandoned centralized institutions for local patronage networks; Egyptian pharaonic ideology absorbed cosmic disorder through the Osiris-Seth cycle narrative, permitting 'restoration' rather than collapse narratives", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia"], "excludes": ["Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Hittite kingship was contractual-legal rather than ritually performative and cosmic", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "No eclipse or omen references in contemporary Hittite or Greek texts near collapse horizon", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian New Kingdom showed no capacity to absorb ideological crisis (Amarna period caused permanent collapse)", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Atypical: draws on Mary Douglas/ideology-of-risk tradition; treats collapse as interpretive failure rather than material constraint; intentionally narrow scope"}
